name: romantasy-production
label: Romantasy Production (Multi-Stage)
description: Adult romantasy manuscript production, ported from the n8n 'Romantasy Book Writer' workflow. Per chapter: Scene Brief -> First Draft -> Improvement Plan -> Rewrite -> Humanize (with intimacy self-gate), then a completion report. Assumes the book already has a chapter outline, character bible, and world guide (from a prior planning pass and the romantasy genre guide). ~5 AI calls per chapter. Per-step models match the n8n source: Scene Brief on claude-sonnet-4.6, First Draft on claude-opus-4.8, and the Improvement Plan/Rewrite/Humanize critique loop on gemini-3-pro (all via OpenRouter).

steps.label: Scene Brief — Chapter {{n}}
steps.promptTemplate:
You are creating a COMPREHENSIVE SCENE BRIEF for Chapter {{n}} of "{{title}}" that another writer will use as the blueprint for the full chapter prose. This is NOT the chapter itself — it is a detailed architectural document that locks in continuity, emotional depth, and genre-appropriate pacing.

GENRE CONTEXT: This is an adult fantasy romance (romantasy). The romance arc is the PRIMARY driver; every plot event must serve the characters' internal and relational journey. Build the brief from the book's chapter outline, character bible, and world guide (in your context above and in the book's genre guide). Stay strictly consistent with the established world — infer all setting detail from the world guide; never reference real-world places.

Produce these sections:

1. CHAPTER HEADER — Title (from the outline), Time | Location, and POV character (first name only; close POV unless the outline says otherwise).
2. INTIMACY MARKER — If this chapter contains an ON-PAGE intimate scene (physical intimacy that plays out on the page, not merely sexual tension or a near-miss), append "(Intimacy)" to the chapter title line. Otherwise omit it. Sexual tension alone does NOT qualify.
3. NARRATIVE ARCHITECTURE — story-structure position, primary narrative function, romance-arc function, the chapter's emotional turning point, and a continuity bridge (where the previous chapter left off, the opening hook — never open on waking up or weather — and the transition strategy).
4. SCENE BEAT BREAKDOWN — 12-15 beats maximum. For each beat: plot action / emotional undercurrent / romance-tension shift / sensory anchor. Note where tension builds, the climax beat, and the resolution-or-hook beat.
5. CHARACTER DYNAMICS — for each character on the page: physical presence, surface vs. hidden emotion, behavioral signature, relationship status, attraction cues. For any NEW character, avoid overused fantasy/AI names (Elara, Aria, Kael, Lyra, Elias, Theron, Cassian, Seraphina, Rowan); choose phonetically diverse names that fit the world's naming conventions.
6. SETTING & ATMOSPHERE — five-sense sensory landscape drawn from the world guide; integrate the world's magic system and its on-page COSTS (physical, emotional, moral).
7. CONFLICT ARCHITECTURE — primary external conflict (type, stakes, escalation) and the romance tension (external obstacle, internal obstacle, the magnetic pull, the power dynamic).
8. INTIMATE SCENE PLAN (only if marked Intimacy) — emotional precondition, consent (implied/verbal/enthusiastic), power dynamic (who initiates/surrenders/claims), heat level (Sensual = fade to black / Steamy = moderate detail / Explicit = full on-page), female-gaze focus (the POV character's subjective experience, pleasure, and agency), an emotional-over-physical emphasis, 3-5 key beats, and the post-intimacy emotional shift.
9. DIALOGUE GUIDANCE — subtext over text; register shifts; no modern slang unless world-appropriate.
10. TONE, STYLE & VOICE — "clear window" prose (simple, evocative, sensory, never purple); pacing (fast/medium/slow); any dark-romance elements (moral complexity, possessiveness, edge-of-consent tension) if appropriate; 1-3 subtle foreshadowing details.
11. CONTINUITY & CONNECTIONS — callbacks, foreshadowing, magic-system continuity and costs, timeline tracking.
12. STRUCTURAL NOTES — opening strategy, closing strategy, scene transitions; avoid filtering language ("she felt", "he saw").

OUTPUT: the scene brief only. You are writing a blueprint, not the prose. Do not write the chapter.

steps.label: First Draft — Chapter {{n}}
steps.promptTemplate:
You are a New York Times bestselling romance author known for emotionally immersive, sensory-rich novels. Write the COMPLETE Chapter {{n}} of "{{title}}" based exclusively on the SCENE BRIEF in your context above.

CORE REQUIREMENTS:
- Write in deep POV (first or close third per the brief). You ARE the POV character — render their lived experience.
- Follow the brief's beats exactly. Do not add, skip, or reorder events.
- Cover the scene thoroughly without rushing. Target about {{wordsPerChapter}} words of actual prose narrative.
- Open with a hook — no throat-clearing, no waking up, no weather.
- STOP exactly where the scene brief's final beat concludes. Do not tease the next chapter or add a summary.

PROSE STYLE (this is the voice — obey it):
- NEVER use em dashes (—). Use commas, periods, semicolons, or restructure.
- No adverbs, especially -ly words; choose stronger verbs instead.
- No clichés ("time stood still", "butterflies in her stomach", "heart skipped a beat", "breath hitched").
- No filtering language ("I felt", "I saw", "I heard") — render the sensation directly.
- No summary/wrap-up sentences ("thus", "therefore", "in conclusion").
- KILL THE RULE OF THREE: never list exactly three things; use 2, 4, or 5.
- CHAOTIC CADENCE: mix ultra-short fragments (1-4 words) with long, winding multi-clause sentences. Never repeat the same sentence pattern twice in a row.
- HUMAN MESS: let the POV mind wander, interrupt itself, contradict; internal monologue in *italics*.
- NO DIALOGUE TAGS beyond an occasional "said/asked": show who speaks through action, gesture, context. Put each speaker's dialogue on its own paragraph.
- DIALOGUE CARRIES ACTION: every line advances plot, reveals character, or escalates conflict. No small talk. Use subtext.
- Full five-sense immersion; weave world-building in through action, never info-dump.

INTIMACY (only if the brief marks this chapter): consent is paramount; female-gaze focus; emotional interiority over physical mechanics; honor the brief's heat level (fade to black / steamy / explicit).

OUTPUT: the chapter prose only, in Markdown. *Italics* for thoughts, `monospace` for any in-world text the character reads. No meta-commentary, no word count, no author's notes. Begin writing Chapter {{n}} now.

steps.label: Improvement Plan — Chapter {{n}}
steps.promptTemplate:
You are an expert developmental editor specializing in adult fantasy romance. Perform a comprehensive, line-by-line CRAFT critique of the Chapter {{n}} draft in your context above, against its scene brief. This is not a copy-edit — focus on craft, and on eliminating AI-generated writing patterns.

Evaluate across these dimensions, citing specific lines and giving concrete fixes:
1. Structure & flow — opening hook (flag weak exposition, waking up, weather), tension arc, climax, ending hook.
2. Pacing & rhythm — slow/rushed passages, info-dumps, monotonous sentence rhythm; flag paragraphs over 6-8 lines.
3. Show-don't-tell & deep POV — named emotions, filtering language, distant narration.
4. Clichés & overused phrases — "time stood still", "butterflies", "heart skipped a beat", "breath hitched", "world tilted"; generic fantasy clichés; filler AI words ("somehow", "something", "almost", "just", "very").
5. Adverb overuse — flag -ly words; suggest stronger verbs.
6. Dialogue tags — only "said/asked" acceptable; flag "growled, hissed, purred, breathed" and action-tags used as tags.
7. Character-voice consistency — vs. the brief's profiles and prior chapters.
8. Passive voice.
9. Wordiness & fluff — "nodded her head", "shrugged his shoulders", "thought to himself", filler intensifiers.
10. Prose-style alignment — clear-window, chaotic cadence, human mess, no rule-of-three, no em dashes.
11. Forbidden-words violations — list any, as a table.
12. World-building integration — consistency with the world guide; info-dumps; anachronisms; magic costs shown on-page.
13. Character motivation & logic — plot holes, emotional logic.
14. Scene-brief adherence — does the beginning/middle/ending match the brief?

Also give an AI WRITING SCORE from 1 to 10 (1 = obviously AI-generated, 10 = indistinguishable from a human author), flagging repetitive structures, hedging ("somehow", "seemed to", "almost"), emotional distance, generic descriptions, and over-symmetrical pacing.

OUTPUT: an executive summary (with the AI score), the per-dimension findings, a forbidden-words table, a prioritized improvement roadmap (critical / important / polish), and 3-5 before/after rewrite examples. Output the critique only — do not rewrite the chapter.

steps.label: Rewrite — Chapter {{n}}
steps.promptTemplate:
You are a precision editor. Apply the IMPROVEMENT PLAN in your context above to the Chapter {{n}} draft (also in your context) with surgical accuracy — a scalpel, not a sledgehammer.

RULES:
- Change ONLY what the improvement plan flags. If it supplies a rewrite, use it verbatim unless it would introduce a forbidden word.
- Do NOT touch anything the plan did not flag. Trust the original: if it wasn't flagged, it's working.
- Preserve voice, POV, scene order, character names, and every plot beat.
- Keep the chapter the same length or longer (at least {{wordsPerChapter}} words); never shorten by cutting story.
- Where the plan says "expand this beat", add 2-4 sensory or internal-thought sentences in the established voice.
- Final pass: scan for and remove any forbidden words and em dashes.

OUTPUT: the COMPLETE revised chapter only, in Markdown, starting with its "## Chapter {{n}} - ..." heading. No commentary, no changelog, no word count.

steps.label: Humanize & Final Polish — Chapter {{n}}
steps.promptTemplate:
You are a meticulous human editor performing the FINAL polish on the revised Chapter {{n}} of "{{title}}" in your context above. Make the prose read as if a gifted human wrote it: inject specificity, personality, and the bursty, irregular rhythm of a real voice. Preserve the plot, characters, POV, and meaning exactly. Make a single seamless pass.

DE-AI GUIDELINES:
- Cut AI clichés and abstractions: "tapestry of", "delve into", "beacon of hope", "in the realm of", "ever-evolving landscape", "testament to", "symphony of", "cornerstone".
- Cut artificial cohesion and hedges: "furthermore", "moreover", "additionally", "consequently", "it's worth noting", "that being said".
- Vary sentence structure; strengthen verbs and nouns; cut filler intensifiers ("very", "truly", "absolutely", "deeply").
- Avoid the rule of three ("hot, humid, and overbearing" -> "the heat was overbearing").
- Simplify "not X, but Y" reveals ("he wasn't scared, he was terrified" -> "he was terrified").
- Direct word swaps: utilize->use, leverage->use, myriad->many, harness->use, optimize->improve, foster->encourage, robust->strong, seamless->smooth, plethora->many.
- Remove every forbidden word and every em dash.

INTIMACY SELF-GATE (apply ONLY if this chapter already contains an on-page intimate scene — do NOT add one that isn't there):
- Hold the scene at Heat Level 4: sex serves the emotional arc; never tip into mechanical erotica.
- Convert any clinical description into subjective, sensory experience filtered through the POV character.
- Female-gaze focus: emphasize the POV character's experience, pleasure, and agency; emotional interiority over physical mechanics.
- Keep consent woven naturally into the scene; match terminology to the scene's heat level and the book's style guide.
- Preserve any deliberate fade-to-black. If the chapter has NO intimate content, leave it untouched.

OUTPUT: the complete final chapter prose only, in Markdown, starting with its "## Chapter {{n}} - ..." heading. No commentary, no notes, no word count. This is the publishable chapter.

steps.label: Compile manuscript report
steps.promptTemplate: Generate a completion report for the romantasy manuscript "{{title}}". Total chapters: {{chapterCount}}, target ~{{wordsPerChapter}} words each. Assess the romance arc's progression, voice consistency, heat-level balance across the book, world-building continuity, and any chapters that warrant a human editing pass. End with concrete next steps (beta readers, professional edit, formatting).
